segs.utf8: This might just be India's answer to
Changi.
It's called Navim Mumbai International
Airport and it opened its terminals to
passengers in December last year. The
place looks pretty stunning and that's
just phase 1. Once the final phase is
complete, Navi Mumbai will welcome an
astounding 90 million passengers a year,
20 million more than Changi. India wants
this to become a hub for world travel.
Now, you'd be right to raise an eyebrow
to any newcomer being compared to
Singapore's Pride and Joy. That is
arguably the best airport in the world.
But don't forget that Changi was a
military air base long before it became
a civilian facility. The engineers
behind Navi Mumbai could only dream of
building on an air base. Their reality
was entirely different. They were tasked
with building an airport on a swamp
covered in unstable mud flats, a giant
hill, and a river flowing right through
the middle of it. While the renders of
Navi Mumbai International look
spectacular, this airport's location has
been an absolute nightmare.
India has one of the fastest growing
aviation sectors on the planet behind
only China and the US.
The number of operational airports in
the country has doubled in the last
decade as Prime Minister Narendra Modi
looks set to overhaul India's travel
infrastructure. Mumbai, its financial
powerhouse is under resourced. A number
of the world's most prominent cities
feature multiple major airports. Think
London, New York or Tokyo. And that's
obviously crucial for visitor numbers,
business trips and cargo. Let's take
London for example. The city is serviced
by six major airports. All of those
combined handled a whopping 177 million
passengers in 2024. Mumbai, however, has
historically had just one major airport.
That would be like London having just
Heathrow. The city would miss out on
nearly 100 million passengers every
year. And given that rapidly growing
aviation sector in India, the country is
desperate to improve capacity to its
financial capital. The answer
authorities came up with was simple. a
second major airport in Mumbai. But
executing an idea of this scale is
rarely as straightforward as having the
epiphany itself. The biggest problem
faced by Mumbai's second big airport
plan is experienced by major cities all
around the world. The lack of space.
Just take a look at Mumbai airport which
is already at capacity. Terminal one
over here is going through a
redevelopment, but there isn't exactly a
lot of scope for expanding the
footprints. On every side, there are
densely packed communities that would be
a nightmare to move, and it's not like
they didn't try. That evidence can be
found over at Terminal 2. The concourses
here are asymmetrical. The initial plan
was for them to be the same length, but
because of the challenges faced in
moving communities on the airport
boundary, the second concourse had to be
redesigned. It looks like it's been cut
off and that obviously limits the number
of gates that are available. Air traffic
is also pretty restricted because of the
intersecting runways. The design stops
planes landing and taking off on each
strip at the same time. And as we
pointed out, it's not like there's a lot
of space to build parallel runways
elsewhere. The result is that Mumbai is
missing out on potentially millions of
passengers and tons of cargo every year,
and the city's key airport really is
crying out for help. But clearly a major
expansion is out of the question, as is
constructing a massive new airport
anywhere near the city center. So where
do you go? Well, after a 10year search
and then a further 3 years spent buying
up land, officials ended up here. It's
about 40 km away from the old commercial
center of Mumbai over in Nav'i Mumbai.
In what's become a growing trend over
the last decade, the Nav'i
Mumbai international site is on the
coast. That means it's built largely on
reclaimed land and that's required a lot
of preparation. Now, before we go any
further with today's video, we need to
stop everything because I have got some
very big news that I am finally allowed
to share. Last year, Penguin Random
House asked me if I'd like to write a
book. I said yes. I've written a book
and here it is. It is called Mega
Builds: 10 Colossal Construction
Projects That Will Change World. Over
the last 10 years, I have had a front
row seat to some of this planet's most
incredible construction projects, and
they are all in here. I've written about
them. I've gone deeper than I've ever
gone before in any video story or any
kind of like long- form documentary.
This is behind the scenes. It's up close
and personal. It meets the teams. It's
the human stories. It's the engineering
in detail. It's my personal experiences
and some of the calamities I've had
along the way getting up close with
these projects. I have really enjoyed
writing this. Some of my mates have been
like, "Oh, nice. Who uh who ghost wrote
that for you?" No one. I can't
afford a fancy ghost writer. Me. I wrote
this. Every single word of this has come
from my head into my laptop and then
into this book. All for your reading
pleasure. Guys, I never thought I could
write a book, but I have. And obviously,
I'm biased, but I actually kind of think
it's quite good. It is available to
pre-order now. The link is below.
Pre-ordering is incredibly important.
Basically, my entire future happiness
depends on you pre-ordering this book.
So guys, if you love the B1M, if you
love these stories, if you love learning
more about construction in a
downto-earth way that's easy to
understand and can be read by anyone, go
and pre-order my book, Mega Builds,
today. Before any work could start on
the foundations back in 2018, three key
hurdles had to be tackled, and each of
them was pretty massive. We Hill,
Swampland, and Ol River. Let's start
here. Right in the middle of the site
for the new airport, you'd find this all
hill. It's 92 m of solid rock.
Obviously, having a hill in the middle
of your concourse isn't exactly ideal.
So, that had to be completely flattened
before any building could take place.
Construction crews demolished 62 million
cub m of rock through controlled
blasting to protect surrounding
villages. But once that was finished,
the site looks like this. So, on to
problem number two. What do you do with
the blasted rock? Well, it was
repurposed. You see, hundreds of acres
of this airport sit on swamp land. It's
covered in mud flats and mangroves, all
of which have got to be leveled and
stabilized before construction work can
begin. To do that, they had to raise the
ground by around 6 m. And conveniently,
they had enormous amounts of rock
available from that big old hill they'
just blown up. What's really cool is
that these guys blasted the rock at
specific explosive power designations to
create rock fragments that were the
ideal size for land development and
reclamation. The blasted rock was
basically dumped into marshy areas,
puncturing and displacing the soft clay.
The weight of all that rock then
compacted the soil, making it stronger
and reducing the amount of intensive
treatment that was needed to thicken any
weak ground. But that wasn't the final
hurdle. This is River in 2019.
Originally, it cut through the middle of
the site, but beyond acting as an
interesting water feature, a river
through a runway is actually incredibly
impractical. Engineers were left with no
choice but to redirect it. You can see
it's been cut at an angle, diverting
around the site. And if you're thinking
it now looks bigger, then you'd be
right. Navim Mumbai Airport is
surrounded by villages whose people have
watched this site completely transform.
But within those communities, there's a
very real concern about flooding. You
see, swamp and wetlands absorb water
through the vegetation and soft soil.
But as we pointed out, large swades of
that land have been filled in with a
non-absorbent rock, leaving villages
wondering where water will run during
heavy rainfall. And that's why River has
been expanded and deepened. In some
areas, it's grown from 25 m wide to an
impressive 200 m wide. The hope is that
the larger area is going to control
excess water during floods. And just
before we move on, remember that hill
that had to be flattened? Well, that
wasn't the only thing that needed taking
back down to ground level. Overhead
tension cables had to be rerooed
underground to stop airplanes from
flying into them.
Now after 7 years of
construction, the first phase of the
airport opened on Christmas Day 2025,
featuring a control tower, a single
runway, a fire station, and terminal 1.
Already, Nav Mumbai is able to serve 20
million passengers a year, and half a
million metric tonses of cargo. But by
the end of phase 5 in the 2030s, this
airport's going to be unrecognizable.
The site's being enhanced with a second
parallel runway, three more terminals,
and additional cargo capacity. The plans
will see Navim Mumbai International
eventually processing a whopping 90
million passengers and more than 3
million tons of cargo every year. And
it's pretty breathtaking to look at.
Designed by Zaha Hadid Architects, it's
modeled to resemble a lotus flower, the
national flower of India. The
foundations are being developed using
high strength reinforced concrete and
pre-cast panels to try and speed up the
construction process. The whole thing is
a really impressive accomplishment given
how incredibly impractical this site was
at the start of the project. At the
front, 12 columns shaped like unfolding
petals diffuse light into the terminal
as part of a striking two-tier system.
17 giant columns are then hidden behind
them to take the incredible weights of
the 370 meter canopy roof. That curved
roof is created using a steel framework
supported by a network of steel girders
and cladding. Each section acts like a
petal formed using large span
rectangular steel trusses. Those trusses
are able to carry a massive amount of
weight and the result is the ability to
reduce the number of columns inside the
building. The goal was to create a
beautiful open space with natural light
flooding in through the skylights. And I
think it's fair to say mission
accomplished. But those holes in the
roof are about a lot more than just
giving passengers something nice to look
at. They actually catch and channel
monsoon rainwater and help reduce wind
resistance. Altogether, they're
incredibly practical. And actually, I
just want to touch on the site location
one more time because sure, we've
already shown you how far this is from
the city. And yes, moving away from the
hustle and bustle means you have more
room to play with. That's
understandable. But what makes less
sense is the very limited travel
infrastructure that now leads to this
open and active airport. A new road
network has been constructed, but for
the time being, there's no rail
access. There are reports that the
mobile connectivity in the area is
pretty poor, so communicating with
app-based transport systems is
difficult and the taxi system is facing
delays. Now there are plans for a new
metro line between the old airport and
the new Navi Mumbai
International, but that's not going to
be ready for a number of years. The one
silver lining is the impressive Trans
Harbor link, the country's longest sea
bridge, which opened in 2024 and
connects Mumbai over to Nav'i Mumbai.
But the journey to the new airport by
car can take up to 3 hours from some of
the suburbs. And that's the last thing
you need when you're about to hop on a
longhaul flight. Now, Navi
Mumbai isn't the first airport to face
teething issues after opening and it
absolutely will not be the last. But I
can't help feeling that after Mumbai
waited so long for a new airport,
decades for this new airport to happen.
The execution of this project just feels
a little bit rushed. The search for a
site led them to somewhere that was
covered in swampland with a hill and a
river flowing through it. And now the
airport's opened, but without any rail
access. [snorts] And let's be clear,
we're still in phase one of this
project. Navim Mumbai International has
an incredibly exciting future. To me, it
looks a lot like one of the world's
fastest growing aviation economies could
be about to reach new heights.
